input.prompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the
JSON output.

I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,

data.reasoningText:
The user wants a .NET 11 console app that serializes a `Person` record to JSON with PascalCase property names using the built-in `System.Text.Json` serializer.

In .NET 11 (which would be the latest), `System.Text.Json` has a built-in `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` that was introduced in .NET 11 (it wasn't available before - previously only `CamelCase`, `SnakeCaseLower`, `SnakeCaseUpper`, `KebabCaseLower`, `KebabCaseUpper` were available).


Actually, I need to reconsider what naming policies are available in each version. .NET 7 introduced the snake case and kebab case variants, but I'm not certain about when `PascalCase` was added or if it even exists as a built-in policy in .NET 11.

The user is asking specifically for what the framework provides out of the box, and the default behavior of `System.Text.Json` is to preserve property names as written. Since positional records in C# generate properties with PascalCase naming by default (like `Name` and `Age` from the constructor parameters), that might actually be the answer without needing any special naming policy at all. I'm second-guessing myself on how C# positional records actually work. Let me clarify: when you declare `public record Person(string name, int age)`, the compiler generates properties that match the parameter names exactly—so `name` and `age` in lowercase, not PascalCase. The naming convention would typically be to use PascalCase for the parameters themselves if you want PascalCase properties.

Since the user has lowercase parameters but wants PascalCase JSON output, they'll need to apply a naming policy like `JsonNamingPolicy.CamelCase` or `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` during serialization—though I'm not entirely certain if `PascalCase` exists as a built-in policy in .NET, so I should verify that.
